request.question: Which filmmaker, Roland Joffé or Tommy Wiseau, is known for more Oscar-winning movies?

title: The Disaster Artist (film)
The House That Drips Blood on Alex
Roland Joffé
The Room (film)
Best F(r)iends
You and I (2011 film)
The Disaster Artist
Tommy Wiseau
The Neighbors (2015 TV series)
Rowan Joffé
sentences: The Disaster Artist is a 2017 American biographical comedy-drama film produced and directed by James Franco.
Written by Scott Neustadter and Michael H. Weber, based on Greg Sestero's non-fiction book of the same name, the film chronicles the making of Tommy Wiseau's 2003 cult film "The Room".
The film stars James and Dave Franco as Wiseau and Sestero, alongside a supporting cast featuring Seth Rogen, Alison Brie, Ari Graynor, Josh Hutcherson and Jacki Weaver.
The House That Drips Blood on Alex is a 2010 comedy horror short starring Tommy Wiseau.
The film was written by sketch comedy group Studio8.
It first aired October 14, 2010 on Comedy Central and was released online at atom.com.
It was also included as an extra on the DVD compilation of "The Tommy Wi-Show", a web series also created by Studio8 and starring Wiseau.
Roland Joffé (born 17 November 1945) is an English-French film director who is known for the Oscar-winning movies "The Killing Fields" and "The Mission".
He began his career in television.
His early television credits included episodes of "Coronation Street" and an adaptation of "The Stars Look Down" for Granada.
He gained a reputation for hard-hitting political stories with the series "Bill Brand" and factual dramas for "Play for Today".
The Room is a 2003 American independent romantic drama film starring, written, directed, and produced by Tommy Wiseau.

The film is primarily centered on a melodramatic love triangle among an amiable banker named Johnny (Wiseau), his deceptive future wife Lisa (Juliette Danielle), and his conflicted best friend Mark (Greg Sestero).

A significant portion of the film is dedicated to a series of unrelated subplots, most of which involve at least one supporting character and are unresolved due to the film's inconsistent narrative structure.

In an interview, included as a special feature on the DVD of the film, Wiseau briefly describes the title as alluding to the potential of a room to be the site of both good and bad events; according to Sestero, the stage-play script from which the film's script is derived took place in a single room.
Best F(r)iends is an upcoming American comedy thriller film directed and co-produced by Justin MacGregor, written and co-produced by Greg Sestero, as well as co-produced by Kristopher MacGregor.
It stars Sestero and Tommy Wiseau, reuniting them fourteen years after the cult classic "The Room", while also including Paul Scheer.

You and I (Russian: Ты и я ; also known by its working title, Finding t.A.T.u.) is a 2011 drama-film directed by Roland Joffé, an English-French film director who is known for his Oscar nominated movies "The Killing Fields" and "The Mission", depicting a fictionalised version of real events adapted from the novel "t.A.T.u. Come Back".
The film features Mischa Barton, Anton Yelchin, Charlie Creed-Miles, Helena Mattsson, Alexander Kaluzhsky, Bronson Pinchot and Shantel VanSanten.
The film is about a teenage girl, Lana, who moves from a rural town in Russia to Moscow, completely unaware that meeting an internet girlfriend, Janie, will result in a string of adventures.
The Disaster Artist is an award-winning 2013 non-fiction book written by Greg Sestero and Tom Bissell.

Sestero reveals the troubled development and production of the 2003 cult film "The Room" while detailing his own struggles as a starving young actor and his relationship with the mysterious Tommy Wiseau.
The book focuses on the difficulties and odd experiences Sestero had behind the scenes and his unlikely friendship with Wiseau.

A film adaptation of the same name developed by Seth Rogen with James Franco directing, producing and starring as Tommy Wiseau along with Dave Franco as Greg Sestero premiered at South by Southwest on March 12, 2017.
Tommy Wiseau ( ) is a Polish-American actor and filmmaker.
He produced "The Room" (2003), which has been described by many critics as "one of the worst movies ever made" and has gained cult film status.
He also directed the 2004 documentary "Homeless in America" and the 2015 sitcom "The Neighbors".
The Neighbors is a sitcom created, written, directed, produced by, and starring Tommy Wiseau.

In Wiseau's first such creative excursion since his 2003 cult film "The Room", it is set in an apartment building and revolves around the lives of the various tenants and their interactions with the "manager", Charlie, played by Wiseau.
Four episodes were released on Hulu Plus on March 14, 2015.
Two additional episodes were released on May 26, 2015.
Rowan Marc Joffé (born 1973) is a British screenwriter and director.
He is the son of director Roland Joffé and actress Jane Lapotaire, and half-brother of actress Nathalie Lunghi.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

He began his career in television.  His early television credits included episodes of "Coronation Street" and an adaptation of "The Stars Look Down" for Granada.  He gained a reputation for hard-hitting political stories with the series "Bill Brand" and factual dramas for "Play for Today".

He produced "The Room" (2003), which has been described by many critics as "one of the worst movies ever made" and has gained cult film status.  He also directed the 2004 documentary "Homeless in America" and the 2015 sitcom "The Neighbors".

The Disaster Artist | The Disaster Artist is an award-winning 2013 non-fiction book written by Greg Sestero and Tom Bissell.  Sestero reveals the troubled development and production of the 2003 cult film "The Room" while detailing his own struggles as a starving young actor and his relationship with the mysterious Tommy Wiseau.  The book focuses on the difficulties and odd experiences Sestero had behind the scenes and his unlikely friendship with Wiseau.  A film adaptation of the same name developed by Seth Rogen with James Franco directing, producing and starring as Tommy Wiseau along with Dave Franco as Greg Sestero premiered at South by Southwest on March 12, 2017.

You and I (2011 film) | You and I (Russian: Ты и я ; also known by its working title, Finding t.A.T.u.) is a 2011 drama-film directed by Roland Joffé, an English-French film director who is known for his Oscar nominated movies "The Killing Fields" and "The Mission", depicting a fictionalised version of real events adapted from the novel "t.A.T.u. Come Back".  The film features Mischa Barton, Anton Yelchin, Charlie Creed-Miles, Helena Mattsson, Alexander Kaluzhsky, Bronson Pinchot and Shantel VanSanten.  The film is about a teenage girl, Lana, who moves from a rural town in Russia to Moscow, completely unaware that meeting an internet girlfriend, Janie, will result in a string of adventures.

Elchin Musaoglu | Elchin Musaoglu [Guliyev] (Azerbaijani: "Elçin Musaoğlu [Quliyev]" ; born Baku, Azerbaijan 11 July 1966) is an Azerbaijani filmmaker best known for his award-winning movies "The 40th Door" and Oscar contender "Nabat".
Rowan Joffé | Rowan Marc Joffé (born 1973) is a British screenwriter and director.  He is the son of director Roland Joffé and actress Jane Lapotaire, and half-brother of actress Nathalie Lunghi.

Roland Anderson | Roland Anderson (November 18, 1903 – October 29, 1989) was an acclaimed movie art director, famous for receiving 15 Academy Award nominations but never winning an Oscar.  Anderson's first Oscar nomination was for his first film in 1933, "A Farewell to Arms".  A frequent collaborator with Cecil B. DeMille - he worked on "Cleopatra" (1934), "The Buccaneer" (1938) and "North West Mounted Police" (1940) - as well as such other classics as "Holiday Inn" (1942), "Road to Utopia" (1946), "Son of Paleface" (1952) and "Will Penny" (1967).
response.summary_1:
- Roland Joffé: passage [1] states he "is ... known for the Oscar-winning movies 'The Killing Fields' and 'The Mission'"; passage [4] calls them his "Oscar nominated movies 'The Killing Fields' and 'The Mission'." (Conflict: are these films Oscar-winning or only nominated?)
- Tommy Wiseau: passage [2] notes he "produced 'The Room'" and other non-Oscar credits; no Oscar-winning films are mentioned for Wiseau.
- Missing / next-hop retrieval: verify whether "The Killing Fields" and "The Mission" won Oscars (which Oscars and how many) and verify whether any films credited to Tommy Wiseau won Oscars. Useful anchors/queries: search for awards for "The Killing Fields" and "The Mission" (Oscar wins vs nominations) and search Tommy Wiseau filmography for Academy Award wins.
response.hop2_query: The Killing Fields Academy Awards wins The Mission Academy Awards wins Tommy Wiseau Academy Awards wins
hop2_docs: List of Academy Award-winning films | This is a list of Academy Award–winning films.  This list contains many films that won Academy Awards, up to and including the 89th Academy Awards held on February 26, 2017.

Killing in the Name (film) | Killing in the Name is an award-winning 2010 documentary film on Islamist terrorism.  It was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Documentary (Short Subject) at the 83rd Academy Awards on January 25, 2011 but lost to "Strangers No More".
List of Academy Award-winning foreign-language films | List of Academy Award-winning foreign-language films
Stephan Dupuis | Stephan Dupuis is an Academy Awards winning makeup artist who won at the 59th Academy Awards for Best Makeup for the film "The Fly".  The win was shared with Chris Walas.

Alexandre Desplat | Alexandre Michel Gérard Desplat (] ; born 23 August 1961) is a French film composer.  He has won one Academy Award for his soundtrack to the film "The Grand Budapest Hotel", and received seven additional Academy Award nominations, 8 César nominations (winning three), seven BAFTA nominations (winning two), seven Golden Globe Award nominations (winning one), and six Grammy nominations (winning two).

Claudio Miranda | Claudio Miranda (born March 1965 in Valparaíso, Chile) is a Chilean cinematographer best known as the Academy Award-winning director of photography on Ang Lee's film "Life of Pi" and as the director of photography on David Fincher's film "The Curious Case of Benjamin Button", the first entirely digitally filmed movie nominated for an Academy Award for Best Cinematography and an American Society of Cinematographers Award.  He is the second Chilean person to win an Academy Award.

Douglas Wick | Douglas Wick is an American film producer whose work includes producing the Academy Award-winning 2000 film "Gladiator", "Stuart Little", and the Academy Award-winning "Memoirs of a Geisha".
hop2_titles: List of Academy Award-winning foreign-language films
response.summary_2: Per the context, Roland Joffé is credited with the films The Killing Fields and The Mission (described variously as "Oscar-winning" or "Oscar nominated"), while Tommy Wiseau has no Oscar-winning films listed; the retrieved passages do not corroborate or resolve the conflict about whether Joffé's films actually won Oscars.
